text: Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon observes tissue flakes and fluid in the right subhepatic and subdiaphragmatic areas. After suctioning the fluid, the exploration reveals a small perforation on the anterior surface of the first part of the duodenum, with visible red mucosa and gastric contents leaking. The surrounding tissue flakes are cleared, and the margins of the perforation are trimmed, with some tissue sent for biopsy.

The surgeon clears the flakes around the perforation site on the duodenum and begins suturing using 2-0 PDS. Three stay sutures are placed: one at the proximal margin, one through the middle, and one at the lower margin of the perforation. Each suture is taken through and through with deep bites to ensure secure closure of the defect.

The surgeon uses an instrument from the right anterior axillary port for liver retraction, providing exposure to take full-thickness bites through the defect. Three sutures are placed over the defect area. The surgeon selects a well-vascularized and mobile omentum to cover the defect, asking the assistant to hold the omentum in place while securing the first suture knot. After securing the first knot, the omentum remains in position, allowing the surgeon to proceed with additional sutures without instrument crowding.

The surgeon secures the knot and trims the excess suture before taking the second bite. The distal suture is tightened to prevent loosening, while the second suture holds the omentum in place. Care is taken to ensure all knots sit properly on the omentum without compromising its blood supply. After suturing, thorough peritoneal lavage is performed under laparoscopic vision.

The surgeon observes unduly dilated small bowel loops, a common occurrence due to paralytic ileus from perforated peritonitis. Careful bowel retraction is performed using atraumatic instruments to avoid damage while preparing for lavage.

The surgeon places two drains after completing the lavage: one drain in the sub-hepatic area through the right anterior axillary port, and another 24 French drain into the pelvis through the left pararectal five millimeter port.
